mappings.rationale: Directly addresses educational systems, policy formation, administrative functions, and the history of educational philosophies (A, B).
Traces the historical development of higher education from its medieval origins through the modern research university (B.3.c, B.5, B.7).
Ancient Greek education established foundational traditions in Western pedagogy that still shape liberal arts ideals (B.1.d).
Addresses Roman educational institutions and their lasting influence on European schooling (B.1.e).
The Confucian emphasis on moral cultivation through study shaped educational traditions across East Asia for millennia (B.1.b, B.4.b).
Provides context for medieval European education including monastic schools, the rise of universities, and grammar schools (B.3).
Examines Byzantine education, which preserved Greek learning and transmitted it to both the Islamic world and Renaissance Europe (B.2.b).
Explores Islamic educational traditions including the madrasa system and its role in transmitting knowledge (B.2.d).
Renaissance humanism transformed European education by reviving classical learning and redefining the purpose of schooling (B.5).
The Reformation reshaped education by promoting vernacular literacy and creating new school systems across Protestant Europe (B.5.d).
Addresses Counter-Reformation educational initiatives including the Jesuit school network and missionary education abroad (B.5.d).
Enlightenment thinkers reconceived education as a vehicle for human progress, ideas that drove 18th-century reform movements (B.6.b).
Rousseau's Emile remains one of the most influential works on education ever written, shaping progressive pedagogy for centuries (B.6.b).
Traces humanistic educational philosophy from the Renaissance emphasis on classical texts through modern secular approaches to moral and civic education (B.5, B.8.b).
Traces China's dramatic educational transformation from the imperial examination system to mass modern schooling (B.8.c).
Examines Indian educational modernization from colonial English-medium schooling to post-independence expansion (B.4.a, B.7.d).
Japan's rapid educational modernization after the Meiji Restoration is a key case study in state-directed educational reform (B.4.c, B.7.d).
Decolonization raised urgent questions about replacing colonial education with systems serving newly independent nations (B.8.d).
Provides historical context for African educational development from indigenous traditions through colonial schooling and post-independence modernization (B.8.d).
Industrialization created new demands for literacy and technical training that drove 19th-century educational expansion (B.7.a).
Illustrates how political ideology shapes educational policy, from Soviet-era schooling to socialist critiques of class-based education (A.3.d).
Addresses Catholic educational traditions and the role of sectarian schooling in shaping educational systems worldwide (A.3.e).
Puritan emphasis on Bible literacy drove the founding of schools and colleges in early New England (B.6.c).
Provides context for how European educational models were transplanted and adapted in the New World (B.6.c).
Globalization has intensified the spread of educational practices across cultures and the rise of international assessment frameworks (B.8).
Theories of learning -- from behaviorism to constructivism -- have shaped educational practices and pedagogical reforms across eras (B.6.b, B.7.b).
Explores how evolving conceptions of childhood have shaped what societies expect education to do for young people (B).
Islamic philosophical traditions and Arabic intellectual achievements profoundly influenced the revival of learning that preceded Renaissance humanism (B.2.d, B.5.a).
Gandhi's philosophy of education -- emphasizing self-reliance and moral development -- shaped Indian educational reform and anti-colonial pedagogy (B.7.d, B.8.d).
